question: The sale of CFC, HCFC, HFC, and HFO refrigerants to service or install refrigeration and air conditioning equipment is;
options: banned from use unless you have a recovery machine.
limited by law to HVAC Contractors and/or Mechanical Engineers.
allowed only if there is proof of need.
restricted to technicians who are EPA certified in refrigerant recovery.

question: The system dependent (passive) recovery process;
options: never needs the use of a pump or heat to recover refrigerant.
must use a pressure relief device to protect the technician and equipment.
recovers refrigerant in a non-pressurized container.
can only be performed on a system with an operating compressor.

question: What can CFC and HCFC refrigerants decompose into at high temperatures (e.g. open flames, glowing metal surfaces, etc.)?
What gases can CFC and HCFC refrigerants decompose into at high temperatures, (e.g., open flames, glowing metal surfaces, etc.)?
What is the approximate pressure of a storage cylinder of recovered R-134a that does not contain any non condensable impurities and is stored in a room where the temperature is 80°F?
What is the approximate pressure of a storage cylinder of recovered R-404A that does not contain any non condensable impurities and is stored in a room where the temperature is 80°F?
What is the approximate pressure of a storage cylinder of recovered R-410A that does not contain any non condensable impurities and is stored in a room where the temperature is 80°F?
What is the maximum allowable factory charge of refrigerant for Type I appliances using CFCs, HCFCs, or HFCs?
What is the maximum normal charge of HCFC or HFC refrigerant in an appliance that is allowed to use system dependent recovery equipment?

options: Cool the compressor to force liquid out of the high side.
Heat the recovery cylinder to vaporize liquid refrigerant.
Turn on the defrost heater to increase the refrigerant's temperature and vaporize any liquid.
Pack ice around the evaporator to ensure maximum liquid is available.

question: What must be done to the refrigerant that is vented off the top when filling a charging cylinder?
options: The refrigerant need not be recovered.
The refrigerant must be recovered only if it is a CFC of HCFC.
The refrigerant must be recovered if it is a CFC, HCFC, or HFC.
The refrigerant must be reclaimed or incinerated.

question: What should a technician do if the refrigerant in a recovery cylinder is suspected to be contaminated?
options: Dilute the refrigerant with virgin refrigerant.

question: What type of appliances are technicians certified to charge and open after passing the Type I examination?
options: Appliances containing 5 pounds or less of refrigerant that have been hermetically sealed and charged at the factory.
Split systems containing 5 pounds or less of refrigerant that have been hermetically sealed and charged at the factory.
Appliances containing 5 pounds or less of refrigerant that have an open drive compressor and have been charged at the factory.

question: What type of certification is required for persons recovering CFCs, HCFCs, or HFCs during maintenance, service, or repair of small appliances?
options: Type I Technician or Universal Technician.
Technician who has passed the core exam.

question: When a pungent odor is detected during a sealed system recovery and/or repair which of the following is most likely?
options: The refrigerant should not be recovered.

question: When checking for non-condensables inside a recovery cylinder known to contain only one refrigerant, why should the technician allow the temperature of the cylinder to stabilize to room temperature before taking a pressure reading?
options: To prevent safety valves from purging refrigerant.
It is a quick method of determining refrigerant level inside the tank.

question: When installing any type of access fitting onto a sealed system;
options: the fitting should be leak tested before proceeding with recovery.
it is not necessary to leak test an access fitting.
the fitting need not be leak tested until the total repair is completed.
the system must be pressurized with dry nitrogen before leak testing can be attempted.

question: When is it essential to take measures to help release trapped CFC, HCFC, or HFC refrigerant from the compressor oil during refrigerant recovery?
options: When using system-dependent active recovery devices.
When using passive recovery devices on small appliances with non-operating compressors.
When the refrigerant system has a low side leak.

question: When R-410A is recovered from an appliance, it;
options: can be mixed with either R-32 or R-125a during the recovery process, since R-410A is a mixture of those two refrigerants.
can be mixed with R-134a but not R-22 during the recovery process.
need not be recovered since R-410A is not one of the refrigerants covered by the Clean Air Act.
should be recovered into a recovery vessel that is clearly marked to ensure that mixing of refrigerants does not occur.

question: When recovering refrigerant into a non-pressurized container from a refrigerator with an inoperative compressor it is;
options: helpful to heat and strike the compressor with a rubber mallet.
only necessary to recover as much refrigerant as will naturally flow out of the system.
not necessary to recover since the refrigerant is probably contaminated.
necessary to chase refrigerant from the oil with pressurized dry nitrogen.

question: When servicing a small appliance found to be leaking it is;
options: mandatory to repair the leak within 30 days.
mandatory to repair the leak only when the annualized leak rate exceeds 15% of the total charge.
not mandatory to repair the leak but it is recommended to do so whenever possible.
mandatory to recover all remaining refrigerant and dispose of the appliance.

question: When using a self-contained recovery device on a CFC, HCFC, or HFC system with an operating compressor, technicians must;
options: recover the refrigerant to 5 inches of mercury vacuum.
recover the refrigerant to both 4 inches of mercury vacuum and 80% of the nameplate charge.
either recover the refrigerant to 4 inches of mercury vacuum or 90% of the nameplate charge.

question: When using nitrogen in repairing a sealed refrigeration system, what should the nitrogen tank always be equipped with?

Inspect the Schrader valve core for bends and breakage.
Replace the damaged Schrader valve core to prevent leakage.

question: Which condition can cause excessive pressure on the high side of a self-contained (active) recovery device?

When the capacity of the recovery tank is larger than the charge size of the system.

question: Which condition requires accessing both the high and low side of the system for refrigerant recovery when using the system-dependent (passive) recovery process?

When the compressor does not run.

question: Which hydrocarbon refrigerant is approved for retrofit into existing household refrigerators?

question: Which of the following are included in EPA's definition of a "small appliance?"

question: What should be on and what should be off during vapor removal from a low-pressure refrigeration system?
options: The system water pumps should be on and the recovery compressor should be off.
The system water pumps and recovery compressor should be on, and the recovery condenser water should be off.
The system water pumps, recovery compressor, and recovery condenser water should all be on.
The system water pumps should be off, and the recovery compressor should be on.

question: What should be used to remove ice from sight glasses or viewing glasses?
What should you do after reaching the required recovery vacuum on a low-pressure appliance?
options: Immediately disconnect the recycling or recovery equipment, and open the system for service.
Wait for at least a few minutes to see if the system pressure rises before charging.
Break the vacuum with nitrogen and open the system for service to begin charging liquid.
Pressurize the system with nitrogen and perform a leak check.

question: What steps may be taken to prevent air accumulation into an idle low-pressure refrigeration system?
options: Leave the purge unit on-line at all times.
Maintain system pressure slightly above atmospheric pressure.
Open the air vents on the condenser.
Intermittently operate the system with no load.

question: When does ASHRAE Standard 15-2013 require that each machinery room activate an alarm and mechanical ventilation?
options: When the TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value -Time Weighted Average) is exceeded.
When the UTL (Upper Threshold Limit) is exceeded.
When the COP (Coefficient of Performance) drops 5% of the original value.
When the EEL (Emergency Exposure Limit) is exceeded by 10%.

question: When evacuating a system, the use of a severely oversized vacuum pump could;
When leak testing a low-pressure centrifugal with nitrogen, what is the maximum test pressure?
When must the leak rate of an appliance be calculated?
options: When topping off or recharging a system due to a loss of charge.
Following a retrofit with a new refrigerant.
Following the installation of a new appliance.
When adding refrigerant for a seasonal variance.

question: When should an appliance NOT be evacuated all the way to the prescribed level?
options: When performing a major repair followed by a minor repair.
When the repair is followed by an evacuation of the appliance to the environment.
When leaks in the appliance make evacuation to the prescribed level unattainable.
When the appliance is being disposed of at a later date.

question: Where does refrigerant go after it leaves the purge unit of a low-pressure centrifugal system?
Where does the purge unit on a centrifugal system take its suction?
Where is refrigerant added to a centrifugal machine?
Where is the lowest access point on a low-pressure centrifugal air-conditioning appliance?
Where is the rupture disc located on a centrifugal chiller?
Where should the discharge from a rupture disc be piped for venting?
Where would you place a leak detector probe to check gas leaks into the water box with water removed?
Which component is particularly susceptible to leaks in low-pressure refrigeration systems with open drive type compressors?
Which of the following actions would always be considered "major" under EPA's regulations?
Which of the following always falls under the EPA definition for "major maintenance, service, or repair?"
Which of the following CANNOT be used as a reason to extend the appliance leak repair deadlines?
options: Requirements of federal, state or local regulations make the repair within 30 days impossible.
The appliance is located in an area that is subject to radiological contamination.
A certified service technician is not available.

question: Which of the following indicates air in a low-pressure system?
Which of the following is a reason to take an oil sample?
options: A new filter drier has been installed.
The unit has had a compressor burnout.
The unit is not cooling properly.
Recycled refrigerant has been added to the unit.

question: Which of the following is NOT required for an appliance that is using an ozone-depleting refrigerant to be mothballed (thereby extending the leak repair deadline)?
options: Evacuate the refrigerant from the isolated section or component.
Evacuate refrigerant from the appliance to at least atmospheric pressure.
Remove the appliance and store it in a warehouse at the facility.

question: Which of the following methods can be used to pressurize an R-1233zd system for the purpose of opening the system for a non-major repair?
options: Warming the liquid refrigerant in the system.
Adding compressed air to the system's condenser.
Adding carbon dioxide to the system's condenser and evaporator.

question: Which of the following safety precautions should be adhered to for low-pressure systems?
options: Use gloves and safety goggles when working with liquid refrigerant.
Reset the water freezestat after recharging.
Shut down the compressor before removing the gauge from the low side of the system.
Use a refrigerant leak detector before entering the mechanical room.

question: Which of the following statements about R-1233zd is FALSE?
options: It is a SNAP approved replacement for low-pressure centrifugal compressor systems.
It is being considered for applications where R-123 might have been used.
It is suitable for air conditioning applications where cooling water is used (large chiller applications).

question: Which of the following statements is true of recycling and recovery equipment?
options: It must be tested by an AHRI-approved third party.
It must be equipped with low-loss ball valves and flare fittings.
It must be able to handle more than one refrigerant.
It must be labeled as certified to meet EPA's requirements.

question: Who is responsible for keeping records of all leak inspections and any completed leak repair verification tests?
options: Only the technician that has actually performed each inspection or verification test
The owner and/or operator of the equipment
The EPA leak rate assessment division

question: Why do chillers using low-pressure refrigerants require purge units?
options: These systems require the removal of contaminated refrigerant.
These systems operate below atmospheric pressure.
Purge units remove refrigerant from the oil sump.
These systems require the removal of condensables from the chiller.

question: Why must water be circulated through a chiller during refrigerant evacuation?
options: To speed up the recovery process.
To prevent loss of refrigerant to the atmosphere.
To prevent freezing of water in the appliance.
To maintain constant refrigerant pressure in the appliance.

question: Why should the oil be removed from a low-pressure system at 130°F?
options: 130°F is the normal operating temperature for oil in a low-pressure system.
Less refrigerant will be contained in the oil at the higher temperature.
Warmer oil has a lower viscosity and flows more easily.
It shows that the heater is working.
